political.s: McDonald's operates a bipartisan PAC that contributed $234,800 to federal candidates in the 2023–2024 cycle, with FEC data showing a bipartisan lean (roughly 46% Republican, 54% Democrat split on $443K raised). The company spent $2.95 million on federal lobbying in 2024, focusing on franchise, labor, and food policy issues. Shareholders narrowly failed to pass a proposal requiring fuller lobbying disclosure, with critics noting incomplete state-level and indirect lobbying transparency.

charity.s: McDonald's committed $20 million to Ronald McDonald House Charities (RMHC) in 2024 as part of a five-year, $100 million pledge. U.S. customers alone donated a record-breaking $73 million through Round-Up for RMHC in 2024, providing over 700,000 overnight stays to families with hospitalized children. McDonald's and its ecosystem have donated over $1 billion to RMHC since 1974.
charity.focus: Children's health, family support (Ronald McDonald House Charities)

environment.s: McDonald's reported total greenhouse gas emissions of approximately 60.2 billion kg CO2e in 2024, with over 99% coming from Scope 3 (supply chain) emissions. The company holds SBTi-validated targets to cut absolute Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions 50.4% by 2030 and reach net-zero by 2050. While it has made progress on sustainable packaging (90.93% toward its renewable/recycled goal) and achieved the largest VPPA renewable energy portfolio in the QSR industry, its massive agricultural footprint and franchise governance gap remain significant challenges.

labor.s: McDonald's has faced persistent NLRB complaints and Fight for $15 labor actions, with the NLRB's largest-ever adjudicated case involving allegations that McDonald's was a joint employer liable for franchisee labor violations; the case settled in 2019 without admission of wrongdoing. Ongoing franchise wage theft class action lawsuits were filed in New York in 2024, and some supply chain suppliers have been linked to forced labor conditions. McDonald's reports achieving gender pay equity at all levels and in every market as of 2024, but worker advocacy concerns remain widespread across its franchise system.

dei.s: In January 2025, McDonald's rolled back key DEI commitments: it retired aspirational representation goals for senior leadership, ended its supplier DEI pledge, rebranded its diversity team as the 'Global Inclusion Team,' and paused participation in external surveys including the HRC Corporate Equality Index. The rollback followed a comprehensive civil rights audit and legal pressure from the 2023 Supreme Court affirmative action ruling, as well as activist pressure from conservative groups. McDonald's maintains it will continue annual demographic reporting and pay equity commitments, and noted 30%+ of its U.S. leaders come from underrepresented groups.

animals.s: McDonald's is not a cosmetics or consumer goods company and does not conduct product animal testing; however, as a meat-centric food company, billions of animals are raised and slaughtered for its supply chain annually. The company has made meaningful supply chain welfare commitments: it achieved all eight Broiler Welfare Commitments across in-scope markets by end of 2024, completed the phase-out of gestation stalls for pregnant sows in its U.S. pork supply, and transitioned to 100% cage-free eggs in the U.S. ahead of its 2025 deadline. Critics note these standards still fall short of the strictest animal welfare benchmarks and that fast-growth chicken breeds remain a concern.

guns.s: McDonald's is a quick-service restaurant chain and does not manufacture or sell firearms. The company has no known policy on firearms carry in its restaurants beyond applicable local laws, and it is not a material consideration in its business model.

privacy.s: McDonald's collects extensive customer data through its app, website, and restaurant interactions, including transaction data, location, and behavioral data for targeted advertising purposes. The company states it does not sell personal information but shares data within the 'McDonald's Family' and with third-party vendors. McDonald's does not respond to browser Do Not Track signals, and its privacy policies exclude franchisee-operated locations, creating significant data governance gaps for a majority-franchised system.

execPay.s: McDonald's CEO Chris Kempczinski received total compensation of approximately $18.2 million in fiscal year 2024. The company disclosed a CEO-to-median-worker pay ratio of 1,014:1, with the median employee identified as a restaurant worker in Poland earning $17,492 annually. This ratio, while lower than McDonald's historical highs, remains among the most extreme in the restaurant industry and reflects the company's heavily franchised, globally dispersed workforce.

sec.note: Pay ratio extracted from proxy filing — verify manually for accuracy
